interactions.summary: Jiang continues the Hannibal answer: armies do not win wars, nations do; Rome's unity beats Carthage's merchant politics, and Carthage's failure to recognize Rome's threat ends in Rome's all-or-nothing destruction of the city.
Jiang checks for more questions and an unclear audience response says 'Good.'
Jiang appears to answer a final prompt about public virtue or why people came to Rome, arguing that most people did not want Roman culture; Rome made people Roman through conquest.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang answering an unclear audience prompt

speaker_notes.note: The final prompt is mostly uncaptured. 'Public virtue. Yeah.' suggests Jiang is responding to an audience term or question, but there is no substantive question text to quote.

claims.claim: Jiang states the strategic lesson as 'armies don't win wars; nations win wars,' contrasting Hannibal's genius with Rome's larger social and institutional capacity.
Carthage undermines Hannibal because its merchant rulers see him as a political and financial threat even while he is trying to save Carthage.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical interpretation of the Second Punic War

claims.claim: Rome is presented as unusually united, unlike Greece and Carthage, whose political factions undermine collective war-making.
Cato the Elder's shock at a wealthy, recovered Carthage leads him to insist that Rome must destroy Carthage before it becomes a threat again.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical interpretation of the lead-up to the Third Punic War

claims.claim: Jiang says Romans habitually blamed enemies for starting wars; against Carthage they created pretexts, demanded weapons, then demanded the city move inland before destroying it.
Roman devotion means all or nothing: Romans neither surrender nor show mercy to enemies.
claims.temporal_scope: Definition of Roman mentality in the lecture

claims.claim: Jiang argues that Carthage's merchant culture run by business people made it unable to recognize the Roman threat that Hannibal understood.
In the final answer, Jiang says people generally did not want to become Roman because Rome was seen as militaristic and barbaric compared with Greek culture; Rome made people Roman through conquest.

signature_moments.moment: The answer to Hannibal is that armies do not win wars by themselves.
signature_moments.source_phrase: armies don't win wars. Nations win wars.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This line generalizes the whole episode from battles to civilization.

signature_moments.moment: Carthage undermines the man trying to save it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Hannibal was being undermined by Carthage itself even though Hannibal was trying to save Carthage
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It sharpens the contrast between a divided merchant polity and a unified Roman war society.

signature_moments.moment: Roman devotion is all-or-nothing and merciless.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It names the dark side of the same virtue that saved Rome after Cannae.

signature_moments.moment: Rome is compared to North Korea: militaristic, barbaric, and unattractive.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The provocation prevents the episode from becoming simple Roman admiration.

signature_moments.moment: Nobody wants to be Roman; Rome manufactures Romans by conquest.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Nobody wanted to be Roman. The Romans made everyone into a Roman through their conquest.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the final twist: Rome's universal identity is not seduction but force.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's model that war is decided by national social capacity, unity, logistics, and institutions, not battlefield armies alone.
The Roman mentality of refusing surrender and refusing mercy to enemies.
Roman senator whose alarm at Carthage's recovery is used to explain the push to destroy Carthage.
Jiang's final contrast: Greek culture attracted people, while Roman identity spread because Rome conquered and made people Roman.

chronology_notes.note: Episode ends by explicitly teeing up the next class on Julius Caesar.

uncertainty_notes.note: Final audience prompt is not captured beyond 'Public virtue. Yeah.'; do not list it as a public question unless future transcript repair recovers the actual question.
